message.content: <local-command-caveat>Caveat: The messages below were generated by the user while running local commands. DO NOT respond to these messages or otherwise consider them in your response unless the user explicitly asks you to.</local-command-caveat>

I know you know quite a lot about this project from CLAUDE.md; imagine that you don't, and run tt/rbw-o.ONBOARDING.sh: is that a good 'teaching guide'?  Check some of the referenced onboarding handbooks from the same perspective.  Thoughts?

addedBlocks:
## vvx
Job Jockey Kit - MCP tools for project initiative management

attachment.content:
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, use Config tool.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- simplify: Review changed code for reuse, quality, and efficiency, then fix any issues found.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (routines) on a cron schedule or once at a specific time. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- rbk-prep-release: Prepare release candidate for upstream delivery
- vvc-BREAK-LOCK: Force-break a stuck VVC lock after a crash.
- vvc-commit: Guarded git commit
- cma-prep-pr: Prepare branch for upstream PR contribution
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

[1;37mPayor — Establish a Manor and Provision the Depot[0m

[0mThe [97;4m]8;;https://github.com/bhyslop/recipemuster/blob/main/README.md#Payor\Payor]8;;\[0m[0m establishes a [97;4m]8;;https://github.com/bhyslop/recipemuster/blob/main/README.md#Manor\Manor]8;;\[0m[0m — an administrative seat[0m
[0mholding the billing account, OAuth client, and operator identity.[0m
[0mUnlike other roles that use service account keys, the [97;4m]8;;https://github.com/bhyslop/recipemuster/blob/main/README.md#Payor\Payor]8;;\[0m[0m[0m
[0mauthenticates via OAuth — representing the human project owner.[0m

[0mBy the end of this handbook you will have a [97;4m]8;;https://github.com/bhyslop/recipemuster/blob/main/README.md#Manor\Manor]8;;\[0m[0m, a [97;4m]8;;https://github.com/bhyslop/recipemuster/blob/main/README.md#Depot\Depot]8;;\[0m[0m[0m
[0mfunded under it, and a [97;4m]8;;https://github.com/bhyslop/recipemuster/blob/main/README.md#Governor\Governor]8;;\[0m[0m service account ready to administer it.[0m

[0mThis ceremony takes about 15 minutes.[0m

[1;37mStep 1 — [1;37mEstablish the Manor[0m

   [0mThe [97;4m]8;;https://github.com/bhyslop/recipemuster/blob/main/README.md#Manor\Manor's]8;;\[0m[0m GCP project hosts the OAuth client and billing[0m
   [0maccount. It must be created before any infrastructure can be[0m
   [0mprovisioned.[0m

   [0mRun the guided setup:[0m
   [0m  [36mtt/rbw-gPE.PayorEstablish.sh[0m[0m

   [0mThis guides you through creating the [97;4m]8;;https://github.com/bhyslop/recipemuster/blob/main/README.md#Manor\Manor's]8;;\[0m[0m GCP project,[0m
   [0menabling billing, and configuring the OAuth consent screen. The [97;4m]8;;https://github.com/bhyslop/recipemuster/blob/main/README.md#Manor\Manor]8;;\[0m[0m[0m
   [0midentity is recorded in [97;4m]8;;https://github.com/bhyslop/recipemuster/blob/main/README.md#RBRP\RBRP]8;;\[0m[0m.[0m

[1;37mStep 2 — [1;37mInstall OAuth credentials[0m

   [0mStep 1 ended with downloading a JSON client secret file from the[0m
   [0mOAuth client you just created. Install it:[0m

   [0m  [36mtt/rbw-gPI.PayorInstall.sh ${HOME}/Downloads/client_secret_*.json[0m[0m

   [0mThis walks you through the OAuth authorization flow and stores[0m
   [0mthe credential securely.[0m

   [0mIf you are refreshing an existing credential that has expired:[0m
   [0m  [36mtt/rbw-gPR.PayorRefresh.sh[0m[0m

[1;37mStep 3 — [1;37mProvision the Depot[0m

   [0mA [97;4m]8;;https://github.com/bhyslop/recipemuster/blob/main/README.md#Depot\Depot]8;;\[0m[0m is the facility where container images are built and[0m
   [0mstored — a GCP project with a container repository, storage bucket,[0m
   [0mand build infrastructure, funded under the [97;4m]8;;https://github.com/bhyslop/recipemuster/blob/main/README.md#Manor\Manor's]8;;\[0m[0m billing account.[0m
   [0mA [97;4m]8;;https://github.com/bhyslop/recipemuster/blob/main/README.md#Governor\Governor]8;;\[0m[0m administers the [97;4m]8;;https://github.com/bhyslop/recipemuster/blob/main/README.md#Depot\Depot]8;;\[0m[0m — creating[0m
   [0m[97;4m]8;;https://github.com/bhyslop/recipemuster/blob/main/README.md#Retriever\Retriever]8;;\[0m[0m and [97;4m]8;;https://github.com/bhyslop/recipemuster/blob/main/README.md#Director\Director]8;;\[0m[0m accounts for those who build and[0m
   [0mretrieve container images.[0m

   [0m[97;4m]8;;https://github.com/bhyslop/recipemuster/blob/main/README.md#Payor\Payor]8;;\[0m[0m creates the Depot:[0m
   [0m  [36mtt/rbw-dL.PayorLeviesDepot.sh[0m[0m

   [0mThis enables APIs, creates the Artifact Registry repository and[0m
   [0mCloud Storage bucket, and configures Cloud Build.[0m

   [0m[97;4m]8;;https://github.com/bhyslop/recipemuster/blob/main/README.md#Payor\Payor]8;;\[0m[0m can list Depots for verification:[0m
   [0m  [36mtt/rbw-dl.PayorListsDepots.sh[0m[0m

   [0m[97;4m]8;;https://github.com/bhyslop/recipemuster/blob/main/README.md#Payor\Payor]8;;\[0m[0m creates the Governor service account:[0m
   [0m  [36mtt/rbw-aM.PayorMantlesGovernor.sh[0m[0m

   [0mHand the resulting key file to the person who will administer[0m
   [0mthis [97;4m]8;;https://github.com/bhyslop/recipemuster/blob/main/README.md#Depot\Depot]8;;\[0m[0m. After this handoff, the [97;4m]8;;https://github.com/bhyslop/recipemuster/blob/main/README.md#Governor\Governor]8;;\[0m[0m can create[0m
   [0m[97;4m]8;;https://github.com/bhyslop/recipemuster/blob/main/README.md#Retriever\Retriever]8;;\[0m[0m and [97;4m]8;;https://github.com/bhyslop/recipemuster/blob/main/README.md#Director\Director]8;;\[0m[0m accounts independently.[0m

   [0mThe [97;4m]8;;https://github.com/bhyslop/recipemuster/blob/main/README.md#Payor\Payor's]8;;\[0m[0m job for this [97;4m]8;;https://github.com/bhyslop/recipemuster/blob/main/README.md#Depot\Depot]8;;\[0m[0m is done unless billing or[0m
   [0mproject-level changes are needed.[0m

   [0mReturn to start: [36mtt/rbw-o.ONBOARDING.sh[0m[0m

[0mThe [97;4m]8;;https://github.com/bhyslop/recipemuster/blob/main/README.md#Sentry\Sentry]8;;\[0m[0m enforces network policy regardless of how it was[0m

The bind[0m
   [0m  lesson is about the [97;4m]8;;https://github.com/bhyslop/recipemuster/blob/main/README.md#Bottle\Bottle]8;;\[0m[0m, not the [97;4m]8;;https://github.com/bhyslop/recipemuster/blob/main/README.md#Sentry\Sentry]8;;\[0m[0m.[0m

[1;37mStep 2 — [1;37m[97;4m]8;;https://github.com/bhyslop/recipemuster/blob/main/README.md#Bind\Bind]8;;\[0m[1;37m the PlantUML [97;4m]8;;https://github.com/bhyslop/recipemuster/blob/main/README.md#Bottle\Bottle]8;;\[0m[1;37m[0m

   [0m[97;4m]8;;https://github.com/bhyslop/recipemuster/blob/main/README.md#Bind\Bind]8;;\[0m[0m mode mirrors an upstream image into your [97;4m]8;;https://github.com/bhyslop/recipemuster/blob/main/README.md#Depot\Depot]8;;\[0m[0m by[0m
   [0mcontent hash. The [97;4m]8;;https://github.com/bhyslop/recipemuster/blob/main/README.md#Vessel\Vessel]8;;\[0m[0m [97;4m]8;;https://github.com/bhyslop/recipemuster/blob/main/README.md#RBRV\RBRV]8;;\[0m[0m declares the upstream digest:[0m

   [0mFor [97;4m]8;;https://github.com/bhyslop/recipemuster/blob/main/README.md#Bind\Bind]8;;\[0m[0m, that means: pull the digest-pinned image from upstream,[0m
   [0mpush it to your [97;4m]8;;https://github.com/bhyslop/recipemuster/blob/main/README.md#Depot\Depot]8;;\[0m[0m under a [97;4m]8;;https://github.com/bhyslop/recipemuster/blob/main/README.md#Hallmark\Hallmark]8;;\[0m[0m tag. No Dockerfile.[0m
   [0mNo [97;4m]8;;https://github.com/bhyslop/recipemuster/blob/main/README.md#Pouch\Pouch]8;;\[0m[0m. No Cloud Build. The image in your [97;4m]8;;https://github.com/bhyslop/recipemuster/blob/main/README.md#Depot\Depot]8;;\[0m[0m is[0m
   [0mbyte-identical to what upstream published at that digest.[0m

   [0mThree [97;4m]8;;https://github.com/bhyslop/recipemuster/blob/main/README.md#Ordain\Ordain]8;;\[0m[0m modes, three trust contracts:[0m

   [0m  [97;4m]8;;https://github.com/bhyslop/recipemuster/blob/main/README.md#Conjure\Conjure]8;;\[0m[0m  Cloud Build constructs the image from project Dockerfile.[0m
   [0m            Trust the build chain (SLSA [97;4m]8;;https://github.com/bhyslop/recipemuster/blob/main/README.md#Provenance\Provenance]8;;\[0m[0m).[0m
   [0m  [97;4m]8;;https://github.com/bhyslop/recipemuster/blob/main/README.md#Bind\Bind]8;;\[0m[0m     Mirror upstream image by digest. Trust the upstream[0m

lastPrompt: I know you know quite a lot about this project from CLAUDE.md; imagine that you don't, and run tt/rbw-o.ONBOARDING.sh: is that a good 'teaching guide'?  Check some of the referenced onboarding handboo…

content.text:
Read as a fresh reader running `tt/rbw-o.ONBOARDING.sh` and then the four handbooks it points at (`Occ`, `Ofc`, `Odf`, `Op`, `Ocd`, `Odb`).

## What the menu does well

- **Concrete value proposition in the first two lines** — "builds container images with supply-chain provenance and runs untrusted containers behind enforced network isolation." A newcomer knows why they're here.
- **Probe-aware status markers** (`[*]` / `[ ]`) on state-affecting prerequisites. A learner knows what's done without trial-and-error.
- **Action-first, term-second bullets** — "Provision the builder toolchain (Inscribe the Reliquary)". Reader sees what the step *does* before meeting the vocabulary. This is a recent improvement per the commit log and it's carrying real weight.
- **Shared-middle pattern for ccyolo/tadmor**: "both start the same way" with common bullets, then the divergence. That's model pedagogy — show structure before variance.
- **Single prerequisite line** at "Director Subtracks" instead of per-track repetition — cleaner, but it does silently drop Airgap's "YFCB required" and Bind's "First Crucible required" hints that used to surface in the menu.

## What the handbooks do well

- **`Occ` Step 7 ("The pattern")** is the best single teaching moment I saw. It shows that all regimes follow `r{letter}r` / `r{letter}v` and lists them in a table. The reader leaves with a *generative rule*, not a memorized list. More handbooks should end with a moment like this.
- **`Ofc` Step 4** — five curl commands with expected status codes (`404`, `403`, `200`, `000`). Theory ("Sentry blocks non-allowlisted") immediately paired with falsifiable practice. That's a real verification, not a wave.
- **`Odb`'s "Mode mixture — name it explicitly"** pre-empts confusion before the reader hits it, and the three-line Conjure/Bind/Graft trust-contract table is the best architectural summary in the whole onboarding. Anyone who reads those three lines understands the shape of the project.
- **Ofc's OAuth-paste regression caveat** citing `github.com/anthropics/claude-code/issues/47745` — current, specific, honest about a wart.
- **HANDBOOK_NAMEPLATE / HANDBOOK_VESSEL exports** reduce copy-paste errors and teach a workflow pattern.

## What would trip a fresh reader

1. **Vocabulary density on first contact.** The menu surfaces ~30 domain terms (Crucible, Bottle, Sentry, Pentacle, Hallmark, Vouch, Plumb, Pouch, Ordain, Conjure, Bind, Graft, Reliquary, Manor, Payor, Governor, Director, Retriever, Enshrine, Abjure, Rekon, Tally, Summon, Airgap, Tethered, Ifrit, Theurge, Kludge, Nameplate, ccyolo, tadmor...) before the reader picks a track. Hyperlinks to README mitigate it, but reading cold requires another window open. A one-screen cheat-sheet view ("first time? run Occ, then Ofc — everything else is optional") could help.

2. **No explicit path-through.** Foundation → Crucibles → Payor/Depot → Director is the implicit ordering, but nothing says "start here if new." A reader could reasonably try `Op` first and hit cloud-cost friction before they've even verified their environment.

3. **Out-of-band file edits are teaching seams.** `Odf` Step 1 ends with *"Open `rbev-sentry-deb-tether/rbrv.env` and set the field, then commit."* That breaks the otherwise-seamless `tt/rbw-cKB.KludgeBottle.sh ccyolo` pattern where the script does the writing. Manual datestamp transcription is exactly where tired newcomers mistype and get stuck. This is the weakest moment in the whole flow.

4. **Foundation section lacks a concept intro.** Kludged Crucibles got a reworked 4-line prose opener explaining Bottle/Sentry/Pentacle; Foundation just jumps into "Configure your Repo's Environment." The Occ handbook teaches regimes well, but the menu doesn't preview *why* these three Foundation items matter as a group.

5. **No time budget.** Ofc's 15-20 min Cloud Build wait is flagged inside the handbook, but the menu doesn't tell a learner "Foundation: 5 min / Kludged Crucibles: 20 min / Your First Cloud Build: 30 min." Time transparency up-front would reduce the "am I doing this right, why is it still running" anxiety.

6. **The Tolkien-esque naming is coherent but opaque.** Every term needs chasing. The project clearly treats the vocabulary as load-bearing (the spec-language approach), and within that frame the consistency is a feature — but the first hour of onboarding is going to include a lot of "wait, what's that word again?" regardless of how good the handbooks are.

## Net read

It's a genuinely good teaching guide — better than most I've seen in open-source infrastructure projects. The probe-aware menus, pattern-teaching (`Occ` Step 7), and verification-via-curl (`Ofc`) are unusually thoughtful. The main drags are vocabulary shock, the lack of an explicit "start here if new" arrow, and the handful of manual-edit seams that break the otherwise tabtarget-driven flow. The last one is tractable — more hallmark-driving or regime-writing commands would close it. The first two are gentle fixes (a header sentence, a time budget) that would meaningfully lower the cold-read cost.

message.content: Make a list of 5 improvement suggestions given your 'as if learning' study.

content.text:
## 5 Improvement Suggestions

1. **Add an explicit "start here if new" path in the menu header.**
   Above the "Foundation" section, add one sentence naming the recommended first-timer sequence: *"New to Recipe Bottle? Run Occ → Ofc, then optionally Ots. Everything below the Crucibles section requires a cloud account."* Right now the ordering is load-bearing but implicit; a newcomer can reasonably click `Op` first and hit GCP billing before they've verified their local environment.

2. **Add a time budget column to each track.**
   One parenthetical per track bullet: *Occ (~5 min)*, *Ofc (~20 min)*, *Op (~30 min)*, *Odf (~30 min, 15 of which is Cloud Build wall-clock)*. Ofc already discloses its 15-20 min Cloud Build wait inside the handbook, but the menu doesn't let the learner plan their session. Low-cost, high-impact: reduces "am I doing this right, why is it still running" anxiety.

3. **Close the manual-edit seam in Odf Step 1 (Inscribe → rbrv.env).**
   Replace *"Open `rbev-sentry-deb-tether/rbrv.env` and set the field, then commit"* with a tabtarget that drives the inscribed reliquary datestamp into the vessel regime, parallel to how `rbw-cKB.KludgeBottle.sh` drives hallmarks into nameplates. This is the one place in the whole Director flow where a newcomer has to transcribe a datestamp by hand — and it's the step where tired learners mistype and get silently stuck at the next Conjure preflight. The seamless pattern already exists elsewhere; extend it here.

4. **Give the Foundation section a concept-intro paragraph, matching Kludged Crucibles.**
   Kludged Crucibles recently got a reworked 4-line opener ("a Bottle runs the workload, a Sentry gates egress, and a Pentacle owns their shared network namespace"). Foundation gets no such intro — it jumps straight into "Configure your Repo's Environment." Add ~3 lines explaining the trio: *repo regime (shared project settings), station regime (per-developer local settings), credentials (the role key the Governor hands you).* Teaches the shape before the bullets.

5. **End more handbooks with a "pattern" recap in the style of Occ Step 7.**
   `Occ` Step 7 — the `r{letter}r`/`r{letter}v` regime table — is the single best teaching moment in the onboarding because it gives the reader a *generative rule* rather than a list to memorize. The other handbooks could each end with an equivalent: `Ofc` could close with the Kludge/Charge/SSH/Quench lifecycle as a four-verb summary; `Odf` could close with the six-ark tour (pouch/image/attest/about/vouch/diags) as a reference card; `Odb` already half-does this with the three-mode trust-contract table but buries it mid-flow. Promote these into explicit "what you now know" closers — they'd turn each handbook from a walkthrough into a retained mental model.

content: Evaluated the onboarding guide (rbw-o.ONBOARDING.sh and its handbooks) from a fresh-reader perspective and delivered 5 improvement suggestions. Next action: await your direction on which suggestion(s) to pursue.

message.content: Lets discuss 3.  This is a tabtarget development, right? I'm musing a new command that takes the new reliquary stamp as an argument plus a vessel name.  Thoughts?

lastPrompt: Lets discuss 3.  This is a tabtarget development, right? I'm musing a new command that takes the new reliquary stamp as an argument plus a vessel name.  Thoughts?

content.text:
Yes, tabtarget development. A few thoughts before you commit to the shape:

**Observation on the two-arg signature.** Existing BUK tabtargets are single-folio by discipline — `rbw-cKB.KludgeBottle.sh <nameplate>`, `rbw-fO.DirectorOrdainsHallmark.sh <vessel>`. The channel vocabulary is `imprint` / `param1` / empty. Two positional args works, but it loses tab-completion friendliness and invites order-confusion ("did I type stamp first or vessel?"). I'd push for a single-folio shape if we can get there.

**We probably can, via fact-file capture.** Ofc already teaches the pattern: Ordain writes the hallmark to `output-buk/current/rbf_fact_hallmark`, and the handbook does `export HANDBOOK_HALLMARK=$(cat .../rbf_fact_hallmark)`. If `rbw-dI.DirectorInscribesReliquary.sh` dropped a `rbf_fact_reliquary` alongside, the new tabtarget needs only the vessel:

```
tt/rbw-rvSR.SetVesselReliquary.sh <vessel>
```

...which reads the fact file and drives the datestamp into `rbrv.env`. Zero copy-paste, matches the Kludge pattern exactly (build → write fact → drive into regime).

**Upstream question worth a minute before building the fix.** RBRV_RELIQUARY lives per-vessel, but the reliquary itself is team-wide state — one inscribe, and *every* cloud-build vessel wants the same value. A single-vessel tabtarget only partly closes the seam; the learner still has to run it N times (or loop). If in practice every Conjure/Airgap vessel carries an identical RBRV_RELIQUARY, the field may actually belong in RBRR (repo regime), and this whole class of manual edits collapses into "inscribe updates RBRR, every vessel reads through." That's a bigger change with migration cost — but it's the root-cause version of the fix.

So three shapes, worst to best:

1. **Your proposal** (two args): works, least disciplined.
2. **Fact-file + one-arg tabtarget**: clean band-aid, matches existing patterns, needs Inscribe to emit a fact file. Scales to "run once per vessel."
3. **Move RBRV_RELIQUARY → RBRR_RELIQUARY**: root-cause. One inscribe, zero per-vessel edits. Bigger surgery.

Which friction is real to you — is the per-vessel copy-paste the actual pain, or is the team-wide-value-in-per-vessel-file the deeper issue? That answer picks the shape.

message.content: Fact file is weak because it is gone after next tabtarget.  No global reliquary now.  Do we have a verb for 'list valid reliquaries'?  I don't think so, maybe not important.  But there should be a check whether named reliquary is valid before attempting to emplace in the vessel.

content.text:
Good catches on both — fact file is indeed ephemeral (output-buk/current/ gets cleared on every tabtarget entry, per handbook line in Ofc), so that design falls apart under normal use. Scratch it.

Given no global reliquary and no list verb, the two-arg shape is the right landing spot. Sketching it:

```
tt/rbw-rvSR.SetVesselReliquary.sh <vessel> <stamp>
```

**Validation is the feature, not the decoration.** The whole point of closing this seam is that a newcomer with a fresh datestamp is exactly the person about to typo it — and without validation, they'd discover the typo 15-20 minutes into a Conjure that fails at preflight. So the tabtarget earns its existence by catching bad stamps before they go into rbrv.env, not by the mechanical sed-in-place. If validation's a no-op, the manual edit is fine.

**What does "valid" mean, operationally?** I'd want your answer here before sketching more. Candidates:

- All four tool images (skopeo, docker, gcloud, syft) present under the expected GAR path for that datestamp?
- Any tool image tagged with that stamp?
- The reliquary namespace exists at all?

The strictest definition (all four present) is closest to "this reliquary will actually work for a Conjure preflight," which is what a newcomer cares about. Cheap to implement if you already know the path template.

**On the list verb.** You're right it's not essential, but here's the thing: if you're writing `is-valid(stamp)` against GAR, `list-valid()` is the same code with `s/match specific/enumerate/`. About 80% free. The case for adding it:

- The failed-validation error message becomes actionable: *"Reliquary r260324193327 not found in Depot. Valid reliquaries: r260324193326, r260301091022."* Without list, the error is just "not found, go re-Inscribe or read your history."
- A learner who ran Inscribe an hour ago and lost the stamp to scroll-off has recourse that isn't re-running a 5-minute operation.

Not required. But if you're in the neighborhood anyway, the marginal cost is low.

**Naming question.** Project voice is verb-heavy (Inscribe, Conjure, Kludge, Abjure, Ordain). "Set" is flat for this vocabulary. "Emplace" (your word) works; "Stamp" is a decent verb-noun match ("stamp the vessel with a reliquary"); "Affix" is archaic enough to fit. Your call, but the handbook will read better with something that matches the neighbors.

So my question back: what's the validation contract? Once I know that, the tabtarget shape is mechanical.

message.content:
Check to see that all expected images are present at reliquary stamp.

I want the reliquary stamp to be first argument, in case we add multiple vessel feature someday, not today.

Validate both stamp and vessel before making any change.

Help me pick an in-metaphor verb for this Director action, ideally without a first letter conflict with other Director actions?

content.text:
Director verbs currently in play and their first letters:

| Letter | Verb | Object |
|--------|------|--------|
| E | Enshrines | Vessel (upstream mirror) |
| I | Inscribes | Reliquary |
| O | Ordains | Hallmark |
| A | Abjures | Hallmark |
| V | Vouches | Hallmarks |
| J | Jettisons | Image |
| r | Rekons | Images |
| w | Wrests | Image |

So taken: **E, I, O, A, V, J, r, w**. Also be aware of mode names (Bind, Graft, Conjure) and cross-class neighbors (Kludge, Tally, Summon, Plumb, Charge) even though they're not strictly Director actions — they'll confuse a reader.

Semantics of the action: the vessel is being *committed to use* a specific toolchain when it next Conjures. Declarative pointer, not a transformation. The verb wants to feel directional: vessel → reliquary.

**Top candidates:**

1. **Consecrate** (C) — *"Director consecrates vessel rbev-sentry-deb-tether to reliquary r260324193326."* Ceremonial voice, sits cleanly next to Enshrine/Ordain/Inscribe/Vouch; they're cousins. C is free among Director actions (Charters is Governor). Risk: minor visual kinship with Conjure in tab-completion (`rbw-dC*`). Strongest voice-fit.

2. **Yoke** (Y) — *"Director yokes vessel to reliquary."* Y has zero conflicts anywhere in the project. The verb is inherently two-argument ("yoke X to Y"), which mirrors the tabtarget signature exactly — you cannot yoke one thing, you yoke A to B. Register is agricultural rather than ceremonial, but the project already spans Kludge↔Ordain, so the range is established. Most distinctive, most memorable.

3. **Dedicate** (D) — *"Director dedicates vessel to reliquary."* D is clean, ceremonial voice (buildings get dedicated). Slightly bland next to the more flavored Inscribe/Conjure/Abjure. Safe but forgettable.

**Ruled out:**
- **Hallow** (H free, but shares root with Hallmark — reader will conflate)
- **Pledge** (P free for Director, but visually confuses with Plumb in `rbw-*p*` tab-completion)
- **Anoint** (A taken by Abjure)
- **Seal/Stamp** (S is Summons across classes; cross-class collision)
- **Bind** (mode name; hard conflict)

My pick: **Consecrate** for voice consistency, **Yoke** if you want the tabtarget to read as inherently relational and be maximally distinctive. If I had to choose one, I'd lean Yoke — the "A to B" shape matches the two-argument signature so perfectly that the verb itself documents the command.

Candidate shape:

```
tt/rbw-dY.DirectorYokesVesselToReliquary.sh <reliquary-stamp> <vessel>
```

or if Consecrate:

```
tt/rbw-dC.DirectorConsecratesVesselToReliquary.sh <reliquary-stamp> <vessel>
```

Which register does the project's handbook voice want to lean into?

lastPrompt: Check to see that all expected images are present at reliquary stamp.  I want the reliquary stamp to be first argument, in case we add multiple vessel feature someday, not today.  Validate both stamp…

message.content: yoke is unencumbered.  Hold on, do we have a rhyming problem with 'enshrine'?

content.text:
No rhyme, phonetically or semantically.

**Sounds:** *yoke* is /joʊk/ (ends like smoke, oak). *Enshrine* is /ɪnˈʃraɪn/ (ends like shine, fine). Different vowel nuclei, different endings.

**Register:** *Enshrine* is sanctification — mirror the upstream image into the Depot's sacred space. *Yoke* is agricultural/relational — bind A to B. They don't sit in the same metaphorical family, which is actually good: the reader won't conflate them.

**Signatures:** Enshrine (`rbw-dE`) takes no folio — it enshrines base images according to regime data. Yoke would take two args (stamp, vessel). Different shapes, different tab-completion neighborhoods (`rbw-dE.Director...` vs `rbw-dY.Director...`).

Handbook prose test:
> *"The Director enshrines the upstream base into the Depot, inscribes the reliquary, then yokes each cloud-build vessel to that reliquary stamp."*

Reads cleanly. Three distinct verbs, three distinct actions, no phonetic or semantic blur.

The only place I could imagine a reader slipping is if they half-remembered "the one about stamping the vessel" and wavered between Yoke and Enshrine — but the stamp/vessel pairing has nothing Enshrine-shaped about it (Enshrine operates on image artifacts, not regime fields), so even that slip resolves quickly.

If you had a specific collision in mind I'm not seeing, flag it — otherwise I think Yoke is clean.

message.content: I was not clear.  I like this idea we have to introduce a director yoke tabtarget to check a reliquary stamp then check a vessel, and if both valid, rewrite the reliquary line.  A very similar problem may exist regarding the step following 'enshrining' a base image for a cloud built image.  You highlighted the reliquary incongruity, but how is enshrine feature today?

lastPrompt: I was not clear.  I like this idea we have to introduce a director yoke tabtarget to check a reliquary stamp then check a vessel, and if both valid, rewrite the reliquary line.  A very similar problem…

content.text:
Good hunt. Enshrine is actually **ahead** of the Reliquary case — it's already self-driving. The relevant moment from `Oda` Step 2:

> *"ANCHOR starts empty because it resolves to the forge's freshly built Hallmark. Re-run Enshrine to populate it ... This time Enshrine sees the internal reference, finds the forge Hallmark in your Depot, and writes the resolved anchor into the airgap vessel's RBRV. Commit that change."*

So Enshrine already does the thing we're proposing Yoke do: it reads the vessel regime, resolves the reference against Depot state, and writes the resolved value back into `RBRV_IMAGE_N_ANCHOR`. The pattern exists. What you were hypothesizing as a parallel seam is mostly absent here.

**The Enshrine model has two flavors:**

*Upstream-origin (Step 1):*
```
RBRV_IMAGE_1_ORIGIN=rust:slim-bookworm
RBRV_IMAGE_1_ANCHOR=rust-slim-bookworm-5ae2d2ef98
```
Anchor is chosen once at vessel-authoring time and committed with the regime. Enshrine mirrors origin→anchor. Idempotent: "already-enshrined bases are skipped." Zero per-Director-session friction.

*Internal-reference (Step 2):*
```
RBRV_IMAGE_1_ORIGIN=rbev-bottle-ifrit-forge
RBRV_IMAGE_1_ANCHOR=
```
Anchor ships empty. Enshrine auto-populates from the latest forge Hallmark in Depot. The Director runs Enshrine, not a separate write-the-anchor command.

**So why can't Reliquary follow this model exactly?** One structural reason: Enshrine has an unambiguous source of truth per vessel (the declared `RBRV_IMAGE_N_ORIGIN` points at exactly one thing; "latest in Depot" has a well-defined answer). Reliquary can't auto-pick, because a Director might have inscribed several times and wants to pin an *older* stamp for a specific vessel build. Human choice is load-bearing, which is why Yoke as an explicit command earns its existence — and why it's not a missing feature of Inscribe.

**The Enshrine seams that remain, in descending priority:**

1. **Manual `git commit` after Enshrine mutates RBRV.** Oda Step 2 says "Commit that change." Same minor seam as everywhere else regime files get touched. Could be auto-committed or transcript-captured. Low urgency; not specific to Enshrine.

2. **First-time anchor naming for upstream-origin vessels.** Someone authoring a new cloud-build vessel has to invent `rust-slim-bookworm-5ae2d2ef98`-style anchor names. That's a once-per-vessel minting task, not a recurring friction, but it's opaque to newcomers. Not your learner's problem.

**Sidebar — I noticed another manual-edit seam in `Oda` Step 4** that's exactly the Reliquary pattern, not the Enshrine pattern:

> *"Open `.rbk/moriah/rbrn.env` and set the Bottle hallmark, replacing the PENDING-ordination placeholder: `RBRN_BOTTLE_HALLMARK=${HANDBOOK_HALLMARK}`. Commit the change."*

This is post-Ordain: a cloud-built Hallmark being transcribed into a Nameplate by hand. There *is* a `tt/rbw-tO.OrdainCycle.sh` that appears to do Ordain + install-into-nameplate as one step (per the tabtarget reference in CLAUDE.md), but the handbook doesn't use it. Either OrdainCycle doesn't fit the airgap flow, or the handbook predates it. Worth checking — it may already be a solved problem the handbook hasn't caught up to.

**Implication for Yoke design:** Build it explicitly as you described (stamp + vessel, validate both, rewrite line). It legitimately cannot ride on Inscribe's coattails the way RBRV_IMAGE_N_ANCHOR rides on Enshrine's — the human-choice requirement is real. And if any code lifts out cleanly while you're building it, there may be a shared "write-a-field-into-RBRV-safely" helper that Enshrine could use too.

toolUseResult.stdout: cat: .claude/jjm/officia/☉260423-1024/gazette_out.md: No such file or directory

content.content:
1	# jjezs_paddock ₣A6
2	
3	## Character
4	
5	Iterative discovery work. This paddock is a living document that evolves through **interview-implement-interview cycles**, not a one-shot design phase. Implementation paces teach us things the interview cannot; interview paces integrate what was learned and unblock the next build. Cognitive posture: patient exploration over productive rush, willingness to revise decisions when implementation contradicts them.
6	
7	Teaching pedagogy is distinct from setup choreography. This heat is about helping learners internalize Recipe Bottle's **novel conceptual apparatus** — vessels, depots, hallmarks, vouching, ordain modes, charters, knights, pentacles, bottles, sentries — not about walking them through prescribed setup steps. Setup steps happen, but they are in service of understanding. General container and GCP knowledge is assumed.
8	
9	## Prerequisites
10	
11	`₣A3` (rbk-mvp-3-onboarding-guides) must complete and retire before this heat races:
12	- `₢A3AAI` (buh-handbook-rename-sweep) — bug→buh module rename
13	- `₢A3AAJ` (rbgm-split-to-rbho-rbhp) — file structure in place
14	- `₢A3AAF` (docs-alignment-and-cross-machine-validation) — final A3 validation
15	
16	After A3 retires, this heat consumes `Tools/rbk/rbho_onboarding.sh` as the home for new handbook content. New tabtargets use a **two-colophon scheme**: `rbw-o` (lowercase, terminal) for the onboarding StartHere entry point, and `rbw-O*` (uppercase family) for individual onboarding handbook tracks (e.g. `rbw-Occ.OnboardingCrashCourse.sh`). This replaces the old `rbw-gO*` onboarding family from A3. The case-split family is a deliberate starting-letter differentiation for clarity. (The initially-proposed `rbw-h?` prefix was found to collide with the existing Hallmark tabtarget family — see Provenance.)
17	
18	## Context — The Malformation
19	
20	A3 produced onboarding guides organized around four "role tracks" (retriever, director, governor, payor). The decomposition followed **authorization boundaries** — which roles can perform which actions. But learners don't come to the tool with a role; they come with an **intent**. They want to explore local containers, evaluate the cloud build engine, set up billing, or administer credentials. Role-tracks answered "what can I do next?" but never "what does this mean?" The teaching was setup-oriented, not understanding-oriented.
21	
22	Breakthrough insight (from the interview distilled in this paddock): **learners decompose along intent and goal, not along authorization**. The same probe can appear in multiple handbooks with different teaching contexts. The same role can appear in multiple tracks for different learner goals. Authorization gates actions; intent shapes learning.
23	
24	## Design Decision Record
25	
26	### Frame 4-refined — Handbook as Self-Describing Document
27	
28	Every handbook tabtarget prints a complete teaching document with live probe results interleaved. Units alternate between:
29	
30	- **Verified units**: teaching prose + embedded probe status (✓/✗ with concrete values) + tabtarget reference to run for fix
31	- **Teaching-only units**: pure conceptual explanation, no probes, no checkmarks
32	
33	A learner reads top-to-bottom for teaching; an expert scrolls for the first red probe; a mid-learner reads around what's broken. **One format, three reading strategies** — no mode switching, no state-based branching logic, no walkthrough-vs-reference conflation.
34	
35	### Probe-Aware Menu Routing
36	
37	`tt/rbw-o.OnboardingStartHere.sh` runs quick triage probes and highlights menu items based on repo state. Unpopulated RBRR highlights the payor path. Populated RBRR plus existing RBRA highlights "find your role." Static menu items remain visible so learners can override routing — probe-aware is guidance, not gating.
38	
39	### Handbook Output Is Ephemeral
40	
41	**Handbook steps don't log.** Teaching output is ephemeral UX; logs are for commands that change state. A `buh_*` display call produces no log file. Commands invoked from within handbook-guided flow still log normally. The split is between **teaching conversation** (ephemeral) and **persistent operations** (logged).
42	
43	### No Step Recording, No Mode Switching
44	
45	Earlier A3 drafts tracked steps taken and supported walkthrough/reference mode switching. Both removed. Retrospection comes from re-running the handbook (which re-displays teaching with current probe state). If a future switchboard layer wants to provide history, it reads logs and probes without any track instrumentation. Tracks stay stateless and idempotent.
46	
47	### Monotonic vs Cyclic Probes
48	
49	Some probes check stable state (RBRR populated, SA files present, reliquary inscribed) — once green, they stay green for a long time. Others check cyclic state (OAuth tokens expire, crucible charge state is transient) — red-after-green is routine. Teaching framing adjusts to cadence:
50	- **Monotonic**: brief "here's what this is" on first encounter, terse diagnostic on re-check
51	- **Cyclic**: "this resets, here's how to refresh" framing, no shame in running the refresh tabtarget
52	
53	### Per-Unit Brevity Discipline
54	
55	Each unit ≈ 3–5 sentences of teaching + 3–6 probe lines + one tabtarget reference. A full track of 8 units renders in ~240 lines — skimmable in 2 minutes, deep-readable in 10. Total track length stays disciplined by per-unit discipline, not by artificial caps.
56	
57	### Teach the Pattern, Not the Inventory
58	
59	The regime landscape has 13 regimes and a consistent `rbw-r{letter}{r|v}` tabtarget pattern. The crash course teaches the **pattern**, not the inventory. Once a learner owns the pattern, they can discover any regime's tools by knowing one letter. Six regimes are user-facing (teach on demand); the rest are deferred or invisible.
60	
61	### Shared Probes, Separate Teaching
62	
63	The same probe appears in multiple handbooks. RBRR probes appear in the payor handbook ("you are responsible for filling this") *and* the director handbook ("your payor established this; verify before building"). Same ground truth, different teaching context. This repetition is load-bearing — different learners in different sessions, each deserves their own framing.
64	
65	### Diagnostic Failure as First-Lesson Principle
66	
67	Running `tt/rbw-rrv.ValidateRepoRegime.sh` against incomplete state **fails diagnostically** — telling the learner exactly which fields need values. This is the crash course's first concrete teaching moment: *"In this system, configuration errors fail diagnostically. Trust the error messages."* Marshal Zero is internal release tooling and must not appear in onboarding content.
68	
69	## Regime Filtering for Pedagogy
70	
71	User-facing (teach on demand):
72	- **BURC** — project-level BUK settings (`.buk/burc.env`)
73	- **BURS** — per-developer station; `BURS_LOG_DIR` is the anchor
74	- **RBRR** — repository regime (depot identity, shared across team, monotonic)
75	- **RBRP** — payor regime (payor's own identity, stable across depot rotations)
76	- **RBRV** — vessel regime (per-vessel image spec)
77	- **RBRN** — nameplate regime (per-crucible deployment config)
78	
79	Deferred / barely mentioned: RBRS (future), RBRM (future), RBRO (invisible validator), RBRA (tooling-created, not hand-edited), BURE/BURD/BURX (infrastructure).
80	
81	Cleanup action items surfaced by interview:
82	- `Tools/cccr.env` — delete (cruft)
83	- `.rbk/rbje_compose_probe.env` — needs scrubbing, out of scope for onboarding
84	
85	## Rough Track Roster
86	
87	Working titles, evocative and distinct from the prior role placeholders. **All names are rough** and will be refined in later interview passes. Each track is a single `tt/rbw-O??.Onboarding{TrackName}.sh` tabtarget (uppercase-O family) rendering a complete teaching document in Frame 4-refined format. Final track acronyms (the `??` slots) are assigned during the drafting pace (₢A6AAA Artifact 3).
88	
89	**Vessel/nameplate assignments**: Tracks that involve hands-on container work should use concrete vessels shipped with the project, not abstract examples. The four shipped vessels and their teaching roles:
90	
91	| Vessel | Mode | Teaching role |
92	|--------|------|---------------|
93	| `rbev-sentry-deb-tether` | conjure (tether) | Sentry firewall image, daily-iteration variant; shared `common-sentry-context/` Dockerfile |
94	| `rbev-sentry-deb-airgap` | conjure (airgap) | Sentry firewall image, airgap-built variant; same shared sentry context |
95	| `rbev-bottle-ccyolo` | conjure | First crucible experience — Claude Code in a sandbox (₢A6AAG creates this) |
96	| `rbev-bottle-ifrit-tether` | conjure (tether) | Adversarial testing vessel — daily-iteration variant, kludge-friendly (`common-ifrit-context/Dockerfile.tether`) |
97	| `rbev-bottle-ifrit-airgap` | conjure (airgap) | Adversarial testing vessel — airgap-built variant, supply-chain disciplined (`common-ifrit-context/Dockerfile.airgap`, FROM forge) |
98	| `rbev-bottle-ifrit-forge` | conjure (tether) | Build-time fixture: warms cargo cache + apt deps for airgap-ifrit; consumed input, not a runtime image |
99	| `rbev-bottle-plantuml` | bind | Upstream image pinned by digest — teaches bind mode |
100	| `rbev-bottle-graft-demo` | graft | Teaching-only graft vessel — local image pushed to GAR, no Dockerfile in project, no nameplate |
101	
102	| Nameplate | Sentry | Bottle | Teaching role |
103	|-----------|--------|--------|---------------|
104	| `ccyolo` | sentry-deb-tether | bottle-ccyolo | First crucible, kludge-only, Claude OAuth subscription required |
105	| `tadmor` | sentry-deb-tether | bottle-ifrit-tether | Daily security test authoring, kludge-friendly iteration |
106	| `moriah` | sentry-deb-airgap | bottle-ifrit-airgap | Airgap chain demonstration target (Building for Crucibles); cloud-built hallmarks; same security suite as tadmor |
107	| `pluml` | sentry-deb-tether | bottle-plantuml | Bind mode demonstration |
108	
109	### Foundation (everyone)
110	
111	1. **Crash Course: Reading the Map** — universal prerequisite. Tabtargets, regimes, log placement, station vs versioned, the `rbw-r{letter}{r|v}` pattern. Local-only, no cloud, no image operations. Teaches BURC, BURS, RBRR at a surface level — enough to navigate the rest. Provisional acronym: `Occ`. **No vessels.**
112	
113	2. **Take Your Station** — joining an existing configured project. Verify your RBRA is placed correctly, run access probes, confirm you can operate in your role(s). Shortest path, mostly probe-driven with minimal teaching. **No vessels.**
114	
115	### Role-intent tracks
116	
117	3. **Kindle Your First Crucible** — crucible explorer. Kludge ccyolo locally, charge, shell in, authenticate Claude Code via OAuth copy/paste between bottle terminal and workstation browser. Teaches: crucible, charge/quench, sentry/pentacle/bottle composition, kludge mode for local hallmark synthesis. *First implementation target — simplest path, zero cloud, tightest feedback loop.* Prerequisite: Claude OAuth subscription. **Nameplate: `ccyolo`.** (Name flagged for "kindle" vocabulary overload — see Open Questions.)
118	
119	4. **Establish the Depot** — payor ceremony (~15 min). GCP project creation, OAuth consent screen, billing, RBRR initial population. Teaches: depot, payor responsibility, consent dance, the relationship between payor identity (RBRP) and depot identity (RBRR). **No vessels.**
120	
121	5. **Knight the Realm** — governor. SA administration: create governor/director/retriever SAs, issue RBRA credentials, distribute securely. Teaches: knight, charter, SA lifecycle, secret distribution discipline. **No vessels.**
122	
123	6. **Receive Your Knighthood** — cross-cutting mini-track for directors and retrievers receiving their RBRA from a governor. Place the file correctly (path derived from RBRR), verify via access probe, understand sensitivity. May appear as a unit within Director/Retriever tracks or as a standalone for experienced users receiving new credentials. **No vessels.**
124	
125	### Director subtracks (dependency-ordered)
126	
127	The director subtracks follow a strict dependency chain: inscribe → tethered → enshrine → airgap. Each track requires the previous track's outcome. The learner builds real shipped vessels — the same ones they already met in the crucible track.
128	
129	7. **Your First Ordination** — conceptual map, **no probes, no vessels**. Teaches the ordination pipeline shape: director pushes a pouch (build context) to GAR, Cloud Build produces an `-image` (the container) and an `-about` (the SBOM), then a separate job produces `-vouch` (provenance attestation). Introduces the tethered vs airgap egress mode fork. All teaching-only units.
130	
131	8. **Inscribe the Reliquary** — hands-on. Get builder tool images (skopeo, docker, gcloud, syft) into GAR. Prerequisite for all Cloud Build operations — conjure's preflight fails without a reliquary. Teaches: reliquary as co-versioned toolchain, `RBRV_RELIQUARY` in vessel regime. **No vessel target** (reliquary is tool infrastructure, not a vessel build).
132	
133	9. **Tethered Cloud Builds** — hands-on, first real cloud build. Tether pool has public internet; base images pulled from upstream during build. Wall-clock ~20 min — pedagogy needs to handle wait time (open question). Requires: reliquary inscribed. **Vessel: `rbev-sentry-debian-slim`** (conjure mode, learner already knows this vessel from the crucible track).
134	
135	10. **Enshrine the Ancestors** — hands-on. Mirror upstream base images into GAR with content-addressed anchors for **all shipped conjure vessels** (sentry-debian-slim, bottle-ccyolo, bottle-ifrit). Produces `RBRV_IMAGE_n_ANCHOR` values per vessel. Enshrine itself requires reliquary tool images. Teaches: supply-chain independence, anchor scheme, air-gap readiness, and the concrete scope of upstream dependencies the project carries. Requires: reliquary inscribed.
136	
137	11. **Airgap Your Builds** — hands-on, airgap pool with no public internet. All dependencies pre-enshrined in GAR. Most restrictive build mode, full isolation. Requires: enshrined base images. **Vessel: `rbev-sentry-debian-slim`** (rebuild the same vessel from track 9, now airgapped — proving the enshrined bases work).
138	
139	12. **Building for Crucibles** — ordain the vessels needed to charge a crucible from cloud-built hallmarks instead of kludged ones. The learner ordains the **ifrit forge** tethered (warming a cargo cache + pre-staging apt deps), then ordains **bottle-ifrit-airgap** on the airgap pool from the enshrined forge, drives the airgap hallmark into the **moriah** nameplate, charges, and runs the adversarial test suite — the same suite that runs against tadmor with kludged hallmarks. Closes the loop: the learner built the airgap security testing infrastructure themselves and validated containment against the same attacks. **Nameplate: `moriah`** (sentry-deb-airgap + bottle-ifrit-airgap). Tadmor remains the daily-iteration nameplate; it is not touched by this track.
140	
141	### Evaluation tracks (split by concern)
142	
143	Evaluators arrive with specific concerns, not a desire to tour everything. Three discrete tracks, each self-contained. **Framing matters**: these learners are evaluating, not executing. Critical eye, breadth over depth.
144	
145	13. **Assay: Supply Chain** — evaluator concerned with build provenance. Tours all three ordain modes using concrete shipped vessels: conjure (**sentry-debian-slim**), bind (**bottle-plantuml**), graft (**bottle-graft-demo** — no nameplate, just a local image pushed to GAR). Teaches: SLSA attestation chain, SBOM via plumb, vouch verdicts per mode (full SLSA / digest-pin / GRAFTED), pouch as security boundary, reliquary as toolchain control. The trust hierarchy across modes is the central lesson.
146	
147	14. **Assay: Containment** — evaluator concerned with runtime security. Tours the crucible architecture: sentry/pentacle/bottle composition, iptables + dnsmasq dual-layer enforcement, namespace isolation. Introduces the Ifrit and Theurge — adversarial AI-authored escape tests that actively validate containment. The evaluator sees the test results, understands the methodology (Claude Code sessions with full visibility authored the attacks, Ifrit delivers them, Theurge coordinates inside/outside observation). **Nameplate: `tadmor`** (the adversarial testing target).
148	
149	15. **Assay: Operations** — evaluator concerned with operational model. Tours credential lifecycle (payor → governor → director/retriever), regime configuration, depot provisioning/unmake, and day-to-day operations (charge/quench, tally/vouch/summon cycle). Teaches: how a small team stands this up, what ongoing maintenance looks like, recovery procedures. **No vessels** — operational walkthrough, not hands-on building.
150	
151	### Cross-cutting surfaces
152	
153	- **Realm Dashboard** — reference surface showing all probes across all regimes. Equivalent to the old `rbw-gOr` reference mode, now in Frame 4-refined format. Reachable from StartHere menu and as its own tabtarget.
154	- **Secret Handling Primer** — the RBRA distribution lesson, appearing as a unit in Knight the Realm (governor side, distributing) and Receive Your Knighthood (recipient side, placing). Teaches operational handling knowledge — *what it is, how to handle, where it goes* — without teaching *how to read*.
155	- **Depot Relationships** — the user flagged wanting good intro text explaining "what a Depot is and how Directors dictate its content but Retrievers use it." This is a relational teaching unit that may appear verbatim in multiple tracks. Candidate for a shared snippet consumed by multiple handbooks.
156	
157	### Remaining gaps
158	
159	- **Graft demo vessel**: ₢A6AAH enrolled — creates `rbev-graft-demo/` with graft-mode `rbrv.env`.
160	- **Ifrit airgap pattern**: decided in ₢A6AAI — shared `common-ifrit-context/` with `Dockerfile.tether` and `Dockerfile.airgap`; three-vessel split (tether/airgap/forge); nameplate symmetry with new `moriah` for the airgap chain. Implementation gated on ₢A6AAv (`ifrit-symmetric-rename` — preconditioning rename sweep) → ₢A6AAV (forge + airgap + moriah + theurge fixture). Trajectory: tether variant retires when kludge cycle tolerates the forge dependency; no ETA.
161	- **Bind mode hands-on**: No director subtrack teaches bind explicitly. Bind is covered conceptually (track 7) and demonstrated in Assay: Supply Chain (track 13). Bind is simple enough (no Dockerfile, no build, just a digest pin in rbrv.env) that the conceptual + eval coverage likely suffices. Revisit if learner feedback says otherwise.
162	
163	## README Anchor Inventory
164	
165	Handbook sequences link into `${RBGC_PUBLIC_DOCS_URL}#{Anchor}` via `buh_tlt`. All anchors below are live in `README.md` as of `6c266d09`.
166	
167	`Vessel`, `Hallmark`, `Vouch`, `Depot`, `Ordain`, `Conjure`, `Bind`, `Graft`, `Kludge`, `Enshrine`, `Reliquary`, `Pouch`, `Tethered`, `Airgap`, `Summon`, `Plumb`, `Tally`, `Levy`, `Payor`, `Governor`, `Director`, `Retriever`, `Charter`, `Knight`, `Nameplate`, `Enclave`, `Sentry`, `Pentacle`, `Bottle`, `Crucible`, `Charge`, `Quench`, `Tabtarget`, `Regime`, `Provenance`, `SBOM`, `RBRP`, `RBRR`, `RBRN`, `RBRV`, `BURC`, `BURS`
168	
169	The `-image`, `-about`, and `-vouch` artifact suffixes are described within the `Hallmark` entry (plain English, no separate anchors).
170	
171	**Not anchored** (intentional): Marshal Zero (internal release tooling), DiagnosticFailure (property not noun), ConsentScreen (Google's term), OAuth (described in prose within RBRP track).
172	
173	## Teaching Voice
174	
175	Teaching content is **vocabulary-first and relational**. The example the user articulated during interview: *"I want good intro text to what a 'Depot' is and how 'Directors' dictate its content but 'Retrievers' use it."* This is the shape of good teaching — nouns introduced in relation to each other, verbs connecting them, roles emerging from what they do to the nouns rather than abstract job titles.
176	
177	Principles:
178	- Introduce nouns in context with concrete referents (a depot IS a GCP project; a vessel IS a configured image spec)
179	- Connect nouns via verbs (directors *ordain* vessels into depots; retrievers *summon* hallmarks from depots)
180	- Roles emerge from actions, not labels
181	- Each new term has a reason to exist at the moment of introduction
182	- Avoid "X is defined as Y" — prefer "when you X, you Y, which is called Z"
183	
184	The load-bearing primitive for vocabulary-first teaching is `buh_tlt` from `Tools/buk/buh_handbook.sh`:
185	```bash
186	buh_tlt "  A " "depot" "${z_docs}#Depot" " is the facility where container images are built and stored."
187	```
188	Renders the noun as a clickable OSC-8 hyperlink to the public-docs anchor. Every new concept introduced to a learner should be a `buh_tlt` call on its first mention.
189	
190	## Release Gate: Agent-Learner Evaluation
191	
192	Every handbook ships with an **agent-learner comprehension test** that functions as a release gate. Design principles (established in interview, mechanics deferred to future pace):
193	
194	- Test for **application**, not recall. Scenario questions, not definition lookups.
195	- **Misconception probes**: questions where surface-level understanding gives wrong answers.
196	- **Differential testing**: run eval against full handbook and against stripped/shuffled variant; signal is in the delta, not absolute score.
197	- **Persona anchoring**: learner agents use a system prompt that reduces inference-from-general-knowledge advantage — *"reason strictly from what the handbook tells you; if it doesn't say it, you don't know it."*
198	- **Synthetic critic alongside learner**: separate agent evaluates the handbook AS pedagogy (gaps, ambiguities, places a naive reader would stumble). Gate requires both learner and critic to pass.
199	- **Goodhart mitigation**: eval questions not in track author's working context; rotate periodically; allow human override with written justification.
200	
201	Mechanics of the eval harness are **deferred** to a dedicated pace in this heat. The eval will co-evolve with the first implemented handbook.
202	
203	## Process Rhythm
204	
205	This heat runs on interview-implement-interview cycles, not linear execution.
206	
207	| Pace kind | Produces |
208	|-----------|----------|
209	| **Interview-paddock** | Paddock refinement + spec for next implementation pace(s) |
210	| **Implement-{track}** | Working handbook tabtarget for one track, tested via agent-learner eval |
211	| **Interview-reconcile** | Absorption of implementation findings + spec for next implementation pace |
212	
213	Interview paces are a **first-class pace type**, not preamble. Each interview pace unblocks N implementation paces; each implementation pace generates questions for the next interview.
214	
215	**Foundation tracks landed**: First Crucible and Crash Course are implemented and working. The cycle has shifted to infrastructure paces (yelp migration, decomposition) that prepare the codebase for parallel director subtrack implementation.
216	
217	## Implementation Progress
218	
219	### Display Layer Migration — Complete
220	
221	Handbook display converted from BUK combinator style to pure yelp style. Canonical idiom (in BCG §Semicolon Sequencing):
222	```bash
223	buyy_text_yawp "  A depot is the ..."; z_depot=$BUYY_CAPTURE
224	```
225	Legacy capture API deleted from `buym_yelp.sh` — zero consumers remained. BUS0 yawp count corrected to 8.
226	
227	**Remaining**: ₢A6AAe converts payor handbook (HREF diastema path), then ₢A6AAf sweeps dead combinators from `buh_handbook.sh`.
228	
229	### Decomposition Plan
230	
231	After yelp cleanup, the monolithic handbook files decompose into one-function-per-file under `Tools/rbk/rbh0/` (₢A6AAh). Enables parallel officia on independent handbook tracks without merge conflicts. Positioned after AAe/AAf/AAg/AAA — sweep, clean, audit, and validate the monolith before extraction.
232	
233	### Critical Path
234	
235	```
236	AAe (payor yelp) → AAf (combinator sweep) → AAg (BCG review)
237	  → AAA (menu cleanup) → AAh (decompose)
238	                            ↓ parallel-capable
239	              AAI→AAv→AAV, AAU→AAV, AAW, AAH→AAX, AAZ, AAM
240	                            ↓ all wrapped
241	              AAF (final docs hygiene — always last)
242	
243	(AAv `ifrit-symmetric-rename`: preconditioning rename sweep between AAI's
244	 design record and AAV's airgap implementation. Parallel-capable with AAU.)
245	```
246	
247	## Open Questions
248	
249	- **Wait-time pedagogy** — what does a learner do during a 20-minute cloud build? Blocking for Tethered Cloud Builds track.
250	- **Agent-learner eval mechanics** — concrete harness design
251	- **Shared snippet mechanism** — how do Depot Relationships and Secret Handling Primer appear in multiple handbooks without duplication?
252	- **Track name refinement** — current roster is rough; final names come from iteration
253	- **"Kindle" vocabulary overload** — overloads BUK/zipper "kindle" (module kindling). BUK usage is implementer-facing so may be tolerable; deferred to track-name refinement.
254	- **Building for Crucibles placement** — does this fold into the crucible track (₢A6AAC/AAD) since kludge bypasses cloud, or is it a distinct director subtrack?
255	- **Crash Course Unit 5 rework** — currently teaches diagnostic failure via Marshal Zero; needs reframing to teach the principle without referencing Marshal (internal release tooling)
256	
257	## Provenance
258	
259	Interview origin: `☉260409-1001`. This paddock is the authoritative digest — officia record identity only, not conversation content.
260	
261	### Lessons
262	
263	- **Mint-time namespace verification** must sweep the existing tabtarget tree (`ls tt/`), not only the paddock's own proposals — a project-prefix check against CLAUDE.md's registry is insufficient when prefixes have families with reserved subfunction letters. (Caught the `rbw-h?` → Hallmark collision; corrected to two-colophon `rbw-o`/`rbw-O*` scheme.)
264	- **Officium identity ≠ officium archive**. Treat `☉` references as provenance markers, not as fetchable artifacts. Capture session content explicitly at the time if it matters beyond committed state.
265	
266	## References
267	
268	### Code and configuration
269	- `Tools/rbk/rbho_onboarding.sh` — home for new handbook sequence functions
270	- `Tools/rbk/rbho_cli.sh` — CLI furnish (sources buh, rbcc, rbgc, rbz, rbho; kindles dependencies)
271	- `Tools/rbk/rbhp_payor.sh` — payor ceremonies (post-A3)
272	- `Tools/rbk/rbz_zipper.sh` — rbw colophon registry; new `RBZ__GROUP_ONBOARDING` added here
273	- `Tools/rbk/rbgc_Constants.sh` — defines `RBGC_PUBLIC_DOCS_URL`
274	- `Tools/buk/buh_handbook.sh` — display module for handbook tabtargets; `buh_tlt` is the vocabulary-first primitive
275	- `Tools/buk/buz_zipper.sh` — generic zipper mechanism (`buz_group`, `buz_enroll`, `buz_exec_lookup`)
276	- `.rbk/rbrr.env` — concrete RBRR example
277	- `.buk/burc.env` — concrete BURC example
278	- `Tools/rbk/rbrp_regime.sh` — RBRP validator (reveals payor identity field shape)
279	- `Tools/rbk/rbgv_AccessProbe.sh` — RBRA consumer (reveals per-role credential file usage)
280	
281	### Specifications
282	- `Tools/rbk/vov_veiled/RBS0-SpecTop.adoc` §Configuration Regimes — regime catalog
283	- `Tools/buk/vov_veiled/BUS0-BashUtilitiesSpec.adoc` §Regime Configuration — BUK regimes
284	- `Tools/rbk/vov_veiled/RBSRS-RegimeStation.adoc` — Station regime spec
285	- `Tools/rbk/vov_veiled/RBSRR-RegimeRepo.adoc` — Repository regime spec
286	
287	### Prior work
288	- `₣A3` paddock — contains the role-track design this heat supersedes
289	- `₣A5` paddock — public-docs refresh; coordinates anchor curation with this heat
290	- `₣AU` paddock — MVP-3 release context from which A3 was spun
291	
292	### External

294	
295	# jjezs_pace ₢A6AAx

Mirrors `₢A6AAu`'s relationship to the first-kludge track: implementation lands in `₢A6AA1`, then this pace absorbs user review feedback. Cognitive posture: responsive, hands-on, driven by user judgment at each step.

308	- Infrastructure from `₢A6AAV` already in place — forge vessel, airgap-ifrit vessel, moriah nameplate, 10 moriah tabtargets, theurge fixture, enclave validator fix.
